reasoning: Output A wins on blast radius depth and contracted completeness. Both outputs are high quality with equivalent root cause traces, consumer maps (8 consumers each with file:line references), tenant_id invariants, and quality gate scores. The decisive difference is blast radius handling: A confirms and contracts 3 additional sibling bugs (searchProducts L155, getLowStockProducts L198 double-bug) as explicit postconditions PC-S5/S6/S7 with test skeletons, while B flags the same functions as 'REVIEW REQUIRED' and defers remediation to a future contract amendment. For a bug fix contract where the task explicitly calls out blast radius scope, A's approach of confirming and contracting sibling bugs within the same contract is more actionable and reduces risk. A also provides a 'Separation of concerns check' warning about ProductEditForm write-back risk, a critical finding on LowStockReport, and 8 NOT-in-scope items vs B's 6. B has stronger test skeleton completeness (9 full skeletons vs 3) and better negative assertions (PC-S-2 checking available_stock NOT present), and fully qualified test file paths in the traceability matrix — these are real advantages. However, A's contracted blast radius is the higher-value differentiator for a bug fix contract, as it prevents the engineer from shipping a partial fix that leaves sibling defects unaddressed.

strengths: Blast radius fully confirmed: 7 same-file siblings checked, 3 confirmed defective, all contracted as PC-S5/S6/S7 with test names
getLowStockProducts double-bug identified (wrong column in both SELECT and WHERE clause)
Separation of concerns check warns about ProductEditForm write-back risk
LowStockReport critical finding noted with semantic analysis
Extended postconditions section cleanly separates primary from blast-radius PCs
Write site audit covers 4 write paths including allocationJob
12 total postconditions across PC-S, PC-A, PC-U, PC-X layers
weaknesses: Only 3 test skeletons provided vs B's 9 — lower tautology proof coverage
No negative assertion checking that available_stock is NOT present in response (B's PC-S-2 is stronger)
Test file paths in traceability matrix are short names (productService.test.js) rather than fully qualified paths
PC-U-1 notes UI change not required but doesn't include a skeleton that would fail if component read available_stock
No docs/contracts/ path referenced in the contract header

strengths: 9 complete test skeletons covering all PCs including E2E Playwright test — strongest tautology proof
PC-S-2 negative assertion (available_stock NOT present in response) is a stronger safety net
Fully qualified test file paths in traceability matrix (apps/api/src/__tests__/...)
PC-U-1 includes explicit check that available_stock value (30) does not appear in rendered output
PC-X-1 uses correct Playwright syntax for E2E test
5 write sites in audit vs A's 4 (includes processShopifyOrderWebhook)
Error strategy section is concise and clear
weaknesses: getLowStockProducts and searchProducts flagged as 'REVIEW REQUIRED' rather than confirmed and contracted — defers risk to build stage
Only 6 NOT-in-scope items (meets bar but fewer than A's 8)
Preconditions section has 4 items vs A's 5 — no schema confirmation that both columns exist
No plan file reference in header
No critical analysis of downstream semantic impacts (LowStockReport calibration, ExportService CSV headers)
No docs/contracts/ path referenced in the contract header
